TSP PLANNING

Optimize Your Thrift Savings Plan

The TSP is one of the best retirement plans available, but most federal employees don't maximize its potential. We help you allocate funds, manage contributions, and plan withdrawal strategies.

CHAPTER I: TSP FUNDAMENTALS

Your TSP Advantage

The Thrift Savings Plan is one of the best retirement savings vehicles available — but most federal employees leave money on the table by not maximizing contributions, choosing suboptimal allocations, or withdrawing funds inefficiently.

Optimize My TSP

"Your TSP is a phenomenal savings vehicle. Don't ruin it with the wrong withdrawal strategy."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • Contribution Strategy
  • Fund Allocation
  • Roth vs Traditional
  • Withdrawal Planning
  • Rollover Decisions
  • Advanced Strategies
CHAPTER II: CONTRIBUTIONS

Maximizing Your Contributions

The TSP offers one of the most generous matching formulas in the retirement industry. Understanding the contribution limits and matching structure is essential for maximizing your federal benefits.

TSP Contribution Limits (2026)

$24,500 Annual Contribution Limit
$8,000 Catch-Up (Age 50+)
$11,250 Super Catch-Up (Age 60-63)
5% For Full Agency Match

From Mike's book: "James Patterson was contributing only 3% to his TSP and missing out on free government money. Increasing to 5% added $2,400 in annual matching funds — a 100% return on investment."

TSP Matching Formula

  • Automatic Contribution: Government contributes regardless of your contribution 1%
  • First 3% you contribute: Dollar-for-dollar match 100%
  • Next 2% you contribute: 50 cents on the dollar 50%
  • Total maximum match: When you contribute 5% or more 5%

The new super catch-up provision for ages 60-63 allows an additional $11,250 in contributions beyond the regular catch-up amount. This creates opportunities for federal employees approaching retirement to significantly boost their TSP balances in their final years of service.

CHAPTER III: ALLOCATION

TSP Fund Strategy

The TSP offers six core funds plus lifecycle (L) funds. Understanding each fund's purpose and risk profile is essential for building an allocation that matches your time horizon and risk tolerance.

G
Government Securities

Guaranteed principal and interest. Risk-free but low returns.

F
Fixed Income Index

Tracks US bond market. Moderate risk, income focus.

C
Common Stock Index

Tracks S&P 500. Core US stock exposure.

S
Small Cap Stock Index

Small and mid-cap US stocks. Higher growth potential.

I
International Stock Index

International developed markets. Geographic diversification.

L
Lifecycle Funds

Target-date funds that adjust allocation automatically.

Sample Allocation Strategies

Strategy G Fund F Fund C Fund S Fund I Fund Best For
Conservative 40% 30% 20% 5% 5% 5+ years to retirement
Balanced 20% 20% 40% 10% 10% 10+ years to retirement
Aggressive 5% 5% 60% 15% 15% 20+ years to retirement

The unique G Fund offers government-guaranteed returns with no risk of principal loss — something not available in private 401(k)s. This makes the TSP particularly valuable for conservative investors and those approaching retirement.

CHAPTER IV: TAX STRATEGY

Roth vs Traditional TSP

The choice between Roth and traditional TSP contributions is one of the most important decisions federal employees face. The right choice depends on your current tax bracket, expected retirement income, and overall tax strategy.

Traditional TSP

  • ✓ Contributions reduce current taxable income
  • ✓ Growth is tax-deferred
  • × Withdrawals are fully taxable
  • × Required minimum distributions at age 73

Best for: High current income, expect lower tax bracket in retirement

Roth TSP

  • × Contributions made with after-tax dollars
  • ✓ Growth is completely tax-free
  • ✓ Withdrawals are tax-free in retirement
  • ✓ No required minimum distributions

Best for: Early career, expect higher tax bracket in retirement

Many financial advisors recommend a "tax diversification" approach — contributing to both Roth and traditional accounts to give you flexibility in retirement. This is especially valuable for federal employees who have predictable FERS pension income and can strategically withdraw from different account types based on their tax situation each year.

22% Current Fed Tax Bracket
12% Typical Retirement Bracket
$30,000 Tax-Free Roth Growth (20 yrs)
CHAPTER V: WITHDRAWALS

Retirement Withdrawal Strategy

How you withdraw from your TSP in retirement is just as important as how you invested during your career. The TSP offers four withdrawal options, each with different implications for taxes, flexibility, and estate planning.

Withdrawal Option Flexibility Income Guarantee Estate Benefits Best For
Monthly Installments High None Remaining balance Most retirees
TSP Annuity None Lifetime Limited Income certainty priority
Single Payment Maximum None Full control Rolling to IRA
Combination Medium Partial Partial Balanced approach

Federal employees who separate from service in the year they turn 55 or older can access their TSP without the 10% early withdrawal penalty. This is a valuable benefit not available in most private sector 401(k) plans.

The key is coordinating your TSP withdrawals with your FERS annuity, Social Security, and any other retirement income to minimize taxes and maximize cash flow throughout retirement.

CHAPTER VI: ROLLOVER ANALYSIS

Keep TSP or Roll to IRA?

One of the biggest decisions facing federal retirees is whether to keep their TSP or roll it to an IRA. Both options have compelling advantages, and the right choice depends on your specific situation and priorities.

Keep TSP

  • ✓ Extremely low expense ratios (0.057%)
  • ✓ Unique G Fund access
  • ✓ Age 55 penalty-free access
  • ✓ Strong fraud protection
  • × Limited investment options

Roll to IRA

  • ✓ Unlimited investment options
  • ✓ Greater withdrawal flexibility
  • ✓ Better estate planning features
  • ✓ Roth conversion opportunities
  • × Higher fees and complexity

There's no requirement to choose one or the other. Many federal retirees benefit from a partial rollover strategy — keeping some money in the TSP for its unique benefits while rolling a portion to an IRA for greater investment flexibility and estate planning advantages.

CHAPTER VII: OPTIMIZATION

Advanced TSP Strategies

Beyond the basics of contribution limits and fund allocation, there are advanced strategies that can significantly enhance your TSP's value over time. These techniques require careful planning but can add thousands to your retirement security.

Advanced Optimization Techniques

Contribution Timing

  • Front-load contributions for maximum growth time
  • Coordinate with FERS pension timing
  • Maximize catch-up and super catch-up contributions
  • Strategic Roth/traditional mixing

Withdrawal Sequencing

  • Coordinate with FERS and Social Security
  • Tax-efficient withdrawal ordering
  • Roth conversion ladders
  • Asset location optimization

The TSP's low fees make it an excellent accumulation vehicle, but its withdrawal options are more limited than IRAs. Many federal employees benefit from a hybrid approach — maximizing TSP contributions during their career, then strategically rolling portions to IRAs for more flexible retirement income planning.

With the average federal employee having $780,000 in their TSP at retirement, proper withdrawal strategy can mean the difference between running out of money and leaving a significant legacy.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

TSP Planning FAQ

Everything you need to know about maximizing your Thrift Savings Plan.

Optimize My TSP

At minimum, you should contribute at least 5% of your salary to capture the full agency match — the government's automatic 1% plus dollar-for-dollar matching on the next 3% of your salary, and 50 cents on the dollar for the next 1%. Beyond that, maxing out your TSP (up to $24,500 in 2026, or $32,000 if age 50+) depends on your overall financial situation, other savings goals, and whether you have high-interest debt. The TSP offers extremely low fees and excellent tax benefits, making it one of the best places to save for retirement.

Traditional TSP contributions reduce your taxable income now but are taxed when withdrawn. Roth TSP contributions are made with after-tax dollars but grow and can be withdrawn tax-free in retirement. Generally, Roth makes more sense if you expect to be in a higher tax bracket in retirement, or if you're early in your career with lower current income. Traditional makes sense if you're in a high tax bracket now and expect lower income in retirement. Many federal employees benefit from a mix of both — we can model your specific situation to determine the optimal split.

The TSP has incredibly low expense ratios and offers unique benefits like the G Fund. However, an IRA rollover provides more investment options, greater withdrawal flexibility, and potentially better estate planning features. If you separate from service in the year you turn 55 or older, the TSP also allows penalty-free withdrawals before age 59½. Whether to keep your TSP or roll it to an IRA depends on your investment preferences, withdrawal needs, and overall estate planning goals. There's no one-size-fits-all answer — we evaluate your specific situation to make the right recommendation.

TSP offers four main withdrawal options: (1) Single payment of your entire balance, (2) Monthly installments based on your choice of amount or life expectancy, (3) Purchase of a TSP life annuity for guaranteed monthly income, or (4) A combination of these options. Each has different tax and income implications. Monthly installments offer flexibility but no income guarantee. Life annuities provide guaranteed income but no access to principal. Most retirees benefit from a strategic combination, often keeping some TSP funds for growth while taking systematic withdrawals coordinated with FERS and Social Security.

L Funds (Lifecycle Funds) are professionally managed portfolios that automatically adjust their allocation to become more conservative as you approach retirement. Each L Fund has a target retirement year (like L 2030, L 2040, etc.) and rebalances quarterly. They're designed as a simple, set-it-and-forget-it option. L Funds work well for hands-off investors who want automatic rebalancing, but they may not account for your specific situation — like whether you have a FERS pension, other savings, or a risk tolerance that differs from the average. We can evaluate whether an L Fund suits your needs or if a custom allocation makes more sense.
